fastapi.q: Give me a high-level tour of the FastAPI codebase as if you're onboarding a new contributor. Cover: top-level directory layout, what each major area is responsible for, and where the core framework code lives (vs docs, tests, scripts, translations). Don't list every file — synthesize the architecture.
Describe FastAPI's project conventions — coding style, type-checking, lint rules, formatting tools, and how dependencies are organized (runtime vs dev vs optional extras). Where are these enforced?
Walk me through how FastAPI publishes a release end-to-end. What CI workflows exist, how are docs (incl. translations) built and deployed, and what security/quality gates run before publishing?

What is the relationship between FastAPI, Starlette, and Pydantic v2 inside this codebase? Which FastAPI features are direct re-exports of Starlette, which extend Starlette, and where does Pydantic v2 plug in?

requests.q: Give me a high-level tour of the requests codebase. Cover: top-level layout (src/, ext/, tests/, docs/), the major modules in src/requests/ and what each does, and how the public API surface relates to the internal layering.
Describe requests' code-quality and security posture: lint/format/type-checking tools and their configuration, plus what security analyses run in CI and how GitHub Actions are pinned/permissioned.

Describe requests' test strategy: what test frameworks/fixtures are used, what's covered by the matrix (Python versions, dependency variations like urllib3<2 or no-character-detection), and how integration with real HTTP is handled.

What runtime dependencies does requests rely on (urllib3, certifi, idna, charset_normalizer) and what role does each play in the request lifecycle? Include the boundaries — what does requests do itself vs delegate to urllib3?

sqlmodel.q: Give me a high-level tour of the sqlmodel codebase. Cover: top-level layout, what each module in sqlmodel/ is responsible for, and how the package bridges Pydantic and SQLAlchemy at a structural level.
Describe sqlmodel's project conventions — code style, type-checking, lint, testing strategy, supported Python versions, and how compatibility with Pydantic versions is structured.

Explain at the architectural level how SQLModel reconciles Pydantic and SQLAlchemy in a single model class. Don't focus on line numbers — focus on which design responsibilities each component carries and how they coordinate.

If I want to add a new feature to SQLModel — say, a new field-level option that maps to a SQLAlchemy column constraint — what files should I look at, what patterns should I follow, and what tests should I add? Treat this as onboarding guidance.
